item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i think about this scip a lot. it's short, but it still manages to be extremely creepy, not just with the anomaly, but with the researcher assigned to it. it seems to me that Dr. Blank is just obfuscating incompetence; why would he send his assistant into the room if not to cover up his own mishandling of the anomaly? he knows exactly what it really does, and he used it to ruin his assistant's credibility so he couldn't rat him out.

though that leaves the question: why? why would he pretend to be ignorant of the anomaly's true effect? perhaps it's pride; after discovering that his initial idea was wrong, he decided he would rather cover up the truth than admit that he didn't immediately understand the anomaly's effect. that's what i think, anyway.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: A replacement image has been found, licenced under Public Domain. I'm awaiting Ihp's approval to upload it. I l've sent him the link.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Sorry, but NC-ND is non-compatible with this wiki. You should remove or replace it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting the source of the image, for staff reference : https://ccsearch.creativecommons.org/photos/2cb5713a-c862-4930-9352-fee3827ba7e1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that the image used in this article was taken by AgentAX and is cc-compliant.

EDIT: It appears the image is non-complaint after all. Replacement of the image is in progress.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the first paragraph of Incident 1310-B-31, Friedman's number was SCP1310-31, without the B, in two places. I went ahead and added the B to those. I hope that's all right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The base idea is interesting but the rampant incompetence on the part of the Foundation is absurd to the point of annoying. If this is how poorly the Foundation oversees a possible hole into an alternate reality complete with it's own Foundation then I'm amazed the whole damn universe hasn't collapsed in on itself. -1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

The actual anomaly of this article seems to be an antimemetic effect, written several years before came into prominence on the site. As such, it would be appropriate to link it to something related the Antimemetics Division Hub, to further this connection.

Editing this:

Due to the gross incompetence displayed by Dr. ████████ regarding SCP-1310, his employment has been terminated as of 4/20/20██. Containment Procedures will be updated in accordance with the findings presented by SCP-1310-B-31.- O5-█.

To this:

Due to the gross incompetence displayed by Dr. ████████ regarding SCP-1310, his employment has been terminated as of 4/20/20██. Containment Procedures will be updated by the Antimemetics Division in accordance with the findings presented by SCP-1310-B-31.- O5-█.

Time expired, edits made as discussed above. Author may revert at any time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the 4/20 reference! Blaze it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would just like to note that I finally got around to updating the cringe-worthy dialog here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slight necropost of a year.

But it wouldn't be erased because it's not on the outside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
slight necropost, but…

A tattoo of your designation is technically written records.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I can get behind the base idea and how the researcher engages in blatant researcher bias, I cannot get behind how the researcher assumes that a certain alternate universe has to have its own version of the Foundation.

That's quite an assumption to make here, and renders an otherwise good development of narrative nearly null and void.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is still something that no one mentioned so far that is left unexplained.

D-Class have their number tattooed on the chest and wrist, wouldn't it be a huge coincidence that all of the D-Class that come out of this room happen to have a number that happened to be currently unused in the Foundation D-Class database?

This is probably one of my favorite articles on site, I really like the original idea behind it. However I will have to downvote because of the inconsistencies. Will upvote if this is fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, this was written back when I was just starting as a writer on the site (this is my third ever written SCP, second successful one), and good lawd is it cringeworthy. I gotta revise that some time, for back then, I was not the Pratchettian Sermomancer I am today, where witty dialogue flows freely from my pen like nectar into the mouth of Bacchus.

(Yes, that was sarcasm; I haven't written with a pen since Sophomore Year of High School. Seriously, though, I will revise this some time soon).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this idea but I'm having trouble getting over the ridiculousness of the doctors dialogue and notes and overall unrealistic interview dialogue

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, some of them are presumably perception altering as well.

item.title: Image Removal
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1310's image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact Fantem or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

Original source:
http://muralmax.com/miami-pediatrics-murals.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From my understanding, the part where people had driver's licences, social security cards, and other identification that SHOULD have had matching entries in databases located elsewhere, what would happen in your scenario is that all physical evidence documenting the D-Class, including the requisition of one meeting your noted specifications, would also vanish.

There would be nothing to compare the emergent person with, because we would have forgotten that we were supposed to compare him with anything, and any evidence saying we should be would be gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it bad that I took the twist completely wrong?

Dr. Blackbox is partially right. The room sends people to another universe. And -31 is right: it erases people from existence.

It takes people from Universe A, erases them from Universe A, and sends them to Universe B. And vice-versa? That was my initial theory after reading.

Now it just seems that it's a room that erases them from Universe A and they walk out confused.
Don't want to spoil anything, so I'll just say that this comes highly recommended by me, so go read it. Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great stuff… but one thing is still poking at the back of my brain here…

Couldn't we test -31's theory, via indirect records? For example,

"A requisition will be sent for a single D-class, male Caucasian between the ages of 40-50. No further details regarding the subject are to be sent to the researcher. At [time], the D-class chosen is to enter the room and close the door. After thirty minutes, researcher is to open the door. If the room contains an instance of SCP-1310-B, and said instance is determined to be a Caucasian male between the ages of 40-50, this will be taken as corroborating evidence for the hypothesis."

It seems a little odd to me that the Foundation wouldn't have conceived of doing something like this. Or would the "wipe" even affect a record even if it's this non-specific?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The real joy of this article is the tunnel-vision researcher and the assistant blowing it all for him. It feels very real and lever. The writing here is very well done.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the SCP and upvoted it. I can see its gone through major rewrites, and it works in its present condition. I hate incompetence, however, and I would presonaly prefer to see Dr. Moron written out altogether. At least he got demoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredible SCiP, Ihpkmn. Incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a fantastic SCiP.

Part of me wonders: was the intent here a retrocausal phenomenon, or simply a world-wide amnesia specific to the SCP-1310-A/-B/-C instance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A most entertaining twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay for the Foundation not being morons. And a nicely done concept. Presumably we're seeing a snapshot of the article in time before the update happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe, it's this. Out of universe, I didn't want to give away the twist before you got to the interview.

As for the D-Class uniforms, Doctor Blackbox just assumed they were D-Class from another Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could be an archived copy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't they have D-class uniforms? Wouldn't they notice the uniform of some guy emerging from the room? Or are uniforms just not a canon thing?

Also, I understand the whole importance of the twist, but if dr. Blank blank is reprimanded for describing it wrong, I doubt they'd leave the article in the database completely wrong as to what it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, excellent article. I just had a similar idea recently, and I found this when I checked to see if anyone else had done it. The only issue I can find is that I feel like the Foundation would be able to inference (perhaps they would have to mention being uncertain due to the nature of SCP-1310's effects) what it does after various attempts at testing; perhaps by checking financial logs, equipment records, living quarter arrangements, that sort of thing, to reveal corresponding errors that indicate non-existent D-level personnel. Of course, with Doctor Blankety in charge, that sort of thing might have been botched. Either way, great job, and upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. I mean, it wouldn't be a situation where some guy from an identical Foundation universe crossed over and this universes Foundation was all like "You're a real hoopy frood who always knows where his towel's at, here, have a job". This guy already has a job on this side, it's just that, through what is essentially an unusually thorough clerical error, all of his records have been lost. I could see the Foundation saying "Well, he's already trained and familiar with our procedures, lets just rehire him and deal with the weirdness resulting from his having been erased as it comes up." *shrug* Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is covered in the article:

The process by which the generation is done is not understood, and attempts to record the inside of the SCP-1310 during the generation process have resulted in failure of all electronic equipment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Possible results:

1) The camera records nothing anomalous (if no people are 'created').

2) The camera records 'obviously anomalous' images from 'the other dimension' of people entering the room (if people were created).

3) "Hey, SCP-1310 just created a camera."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um, put a camera in the room and close the door…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man this is fascinating. I love it. I wish there were a little more of the post-Dr. Blackbox fallout, though. I feel like his actions are pretty bad here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The process by which the generation is done is not understood, and attempts to record the inside of the SCP-1310 during the generation process have resulted in failure of all electronic equipment.

The word 'done' feels a little clunky here.

Otherwise, great article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your input. I edited it a bit… but in the process, I seem to have messed up the code for the rating module somehow. If someone could please fix that, I would really appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, step by step.

SCP-1310 enters an active state when the door to the room is closed for, on average, a span of 5 minutes and 43 seconds.

It seems like this would activate repeatedly, and produce around two hundred people every day. If the door is kept open most of the day (why?), you should mention it in the article.

attempts to record the inside of the SCP-1310 during the generation process have resulted in failure of all electronic equipment

This isn't related to the primary effect in any way, and unnecessary anyway, since the video showing a person walking into the room, waiting and then leaving doesn't provide any new data - Dr. Moron will just assume that the door acts as the portal between universes.

The generated humans will fall under three specific categories … designated SCP-1310-A … SCP-1310-B … SCP-1310-C

I see no reason for designating them separately, since it's not like they have different anomalous effects, but whatever, that's not really important.

Dr. ████████'s request has been approved. Testing is scheduled … Dr.████████ has yet to submit the proper paperwork, or, indeed, take any action whatsoever to conduct his testing

This is not how it would look like in-character. What would happen is that Dr. Moron would submit all paperwork, proceed with test as planned, and just as he is about to begin he will be interrupted by a man popping out of the room. The man will be apprehended as per protocol, and just as Dr. Moron would try to return to the testing, another man would be generated in the room. This would repeat a dozen of times, and then Dr. Moron would finally begin the test only to find out that no one send him any D-Classes for some reason. So the reason for test cancellation would be lack of test subjects, not absence of paperwork.

SCP-1310 was opened for cleaning, and a single specimen of SCP-1310-Cwas found inside

You defined 1310-C above as a clinic personnel, therefore Charles Freedman would not be a -C.

SCP-1310-31 identified himself

If he is a -C, then he would be SCP-1310-C-31, not just -31. Also, 31 seems like a small number, considering that the clinic was open for three month and have been materializing hundreds of people every day.

Also, if you decide to keep -ABCs, you should update the containment procedures to include -Cs.

Really, just changing the activation criteria to random people at random intervals of time, and maybe changing the reason for test rescheduling, would make most inconstancies go away.

I canceled my downvote, because this was well written and twisted the "fabricated people" concept in the direction I didn't expect, but I still can't upvote the current version. The fact that this is currently at +32 may indicate that the fridge logic here is not as apparent as I think it is, so it's your decision in the end whether and how to change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will reread and possibly revote this in the morning, with a clear head. For now I'll just say that saying that the room occasionally produces people at irregular intervals is better than setting some hard conditions, or at least that's how it seem to me at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah. Didn't account for the doctors. That's in there now. Thanks.

To answer your question about the 32-minute thing, it works like this.

Step 1: Person or Persons placed into SCP-1310. For example, a parent and their kid, here for a medical checkup.

Step 2: SCP-1310 blinds us (to the existence of the people within) with science! Eventually, they get sick of waiting, and step out of the room, asking "What the hell is going on?" In the case of D-Class, they may have been sitting in their for hours, and finally get the balls to step outside. At this point, people inquire to what they're doing here, and one of two things happens:

2a: They match the norms for SCP-1310 (i.e. one adult and one child in the room, with maybe a doctor or nurse), in which case, they get sorted all nice and neat.

2b: They do not match the norms for SCP-1310, and, in the case of the D-Class, at least, lie about their identity when asked who they are. Doctor Blankety is a lazy, lazy man who doesn't like extra paperwork and therefore, just waves his hand at them and says "Yes, yes, all right. You're not a parent, a child, or a doctor, you can go." (Let's hope that this guy never got assigned to any Keters…) The only reason that 1310-31 got noticed at all is because he was in the room when they were cleaning it, and wasn't discovered by Doctor Blankety.

…I actually thought I put that part in there. I know I at least saved it as a draft… must've gotten deleted. -_-

And as for the time span… it could theoretically be any length of time, so long as the door's closed. I've changed it to show that the time in the document only reflects the average time between "generations" of specimens of SCP-1310-A , -B and -C.

Hope that answers your questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for plotholes. The main being the 32 minutes thing. How the hell is this supposed to work? We close the door of the empty room for half an hour, no one appears, we send someone in to find out why, and then whoa we didn't send anyone and the room created a person just as planned? But then the periods between creation wouldn't be consistent. And is the person who enters erased immediately? Then why doesn't original descriptions say something like "we never send anyone to check what the room looks like from the inside and I don't have any idea why actually let's send someone right now INSERTMAGICHERE we never send anyone to check what the room looks like from the inside".

And the lack of D-Class doesn't make sense. And doctors. And there were other minor things that I forgot that doesn't make sense when you reread the article already knowing what it does.

I enjoyed the way it was written, however, and even initially upvoted it before reversing my vote. If you could edit it to make more sense, I would change my vote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Speaking from experience here: I have to go to the doctor a LOT for allergy checkups. And, yes, it does actually take that long for him to see me.. I'm lucky to get in and out of there in under an hour, and that's on a good day. I was once stuck in the doctor's office for two and a half hours because the nurse messed up my inoculation; after a while, I started wondering if they even remembered that I was there, which, essentially, is where this SCP came from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but the document says the door has to remain shut for at least 32 minutes and 43 seconds for anything to happen. Hmmm, though I suppose it's possible that parent and child sometimes have to wait 33 minutes or more for a doctor to come see them, but no doctor eve spends 33 minutes or more with a single patient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but the D-class has to come back out the door, where they'll be caught by the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the parent and child go into the room, and are promptly erased from history, then as far as the clinic staff is concerned, nobody's in the room. Therefore, they'll never bother to send a doctor in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think about this for a second, though:

If you were a D-Class, convicted for crimes so awful that they MADE you D-Class, would you want to be remembered? Think about it. Nobody remembers you = potential for a new life. As for the records… Dr. Blankety-Blank is kind of a douche. If something didn't match up with established norms, he'd probably not even record it. He'd just wave them out and say "Yes, Yes, fine. If you don't have a kid with you, then move along."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "erase them" interpretation doesn't match up with the description. First, the room would at least sometimes let out a doctor, in addition to the under 18 patient and over 18 parent/guardian. Secondly, if it lets out patients and guardians after having erased them from the timeline, it would also have let out all of the D-class that were sent into the room, so there'd be a record of the room releasing a bunch of humans claiming to be D-class, and claiming to know the researchers and guards they interacted with, but no one would remember sending them in, and there'd be no records of their existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And he appears to be the sort of person who can't admit he's wrong until his nose is rubbed in it by someone of at least O5-level. (I presume the O5s can read these logs and figure out what's going on.)

I think the article is pretty good, but I also think that Dr. ████████ needs to be smacked in the back of the head. Or at the very least demoted.

At the very least have Dr. BlanketyBlank get demoted or something. He deserves it.

Seeing as this is such a popular idea, I've added a little blurb in the end about him getting demoted. I was tempted to add something about SCP-1310-31 getting a job with the Foundation, but decided against it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the same problem I have with articles like this. The articles are based on the premise that the Foundation can't figure it out while we the audience, ostensibly reading the same report, are supposed to understand. I can't see the Foundation being that stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another thing, there's no way the O-5's would let him use the people "created" by 1310 as D-Class subjects under Protocol England-Somenumber, because that runs the risk of cross-SCP contamination. The foundation isn't stupid, they go through a lot of trouble to get D-Class subjects so something like this probably isn't going to fly. And wouldn't that SCP guy "created" by 1310 have paperwork signed by the O-5 council that contains a code only they could possibly know or something to prove that he's been erased from history and not from an alternate universe?

At the very least have Dr. BlanketyBlank get demoted or something. He deserves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, this isn't a bad idea, and it isn't badly written; but we have a handful of articles with this same punchline of the Foundation grossly misinterpreting obvious reality shift events, and the more I see of them the more I dislike all of them. I just can't swallow the idea that the Foundation could operate at all in the face of such willfull ignorance as this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, according to certain interpretations of quantum theory, Dr. Blackbox isn't that far off - once your existence has been erased from history, you're now occupying an alternate universe in which you never existed…

…unfortunately, he's so incredibly and unutterably wrong about how that functions that it'd be laughable, if it weren't horrifying. And he appears to be the sort of person who can't admit he's wrong until his nose is rubbed in it by someone of at least O5-level. (I presume the O5s can read these logs and figure out what's going on.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those SCPs that works perfectly fine without the twist. And then you get to the twist, want to print it out, and smack M. Night Shamaladingdong in the face with it.

Bravo, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like Document 1310-02 is either superfluous or just really clunky, and you need to go back and look for missing spaces, but whoadamn that last log. This one's finally ready.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last log punched me in the brain. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the article is pretty good, but I also think that Dr. ████████ needs to be smacked in the back of the head. Or at the very least demoted.
